mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: A frosty/ icy start to the day, but warmer in the afternoon when these results were taken. I was stood on the bank of the river, and suddenly saw  a flash of blue on the opposite bank. It wasn't long before it appeared again, and confirmed that it was indeed a Kingfisher. The first one I've seen in over thirty years! Beautiful.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: The river gauge around half a mile downstream showed 36cm (summer 2017 level 18 - 20cm, recent spates 80 - 100cm). Water looked fairly coloured after overnight rain, greenish rather than muddy. Secchi disk just visible with tube full, so < 12 NTU No foam on surface at sample point, but some dirty foam on woody debris downstream.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: The river has been tunnelled and channelled in places in order to build roads and bridges for the surrounding village, as a result the area studied had very steep concrete sides protected by fences, making access to the river difficult. Despite this, the river appeared in good health with clear visibility of the river bed and plenty of visible life, including plenty of fish.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: The colour development of the samples was extremely rapid, both PO4 and NO3 reached the upper end of the scales within 1 - 2 minutes. All the surrounding fields are arable with barley or bearded wheat being grown. The sampling site is around 150 yards dow

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: The colour development of the samples was extremely rapid, both PO4 and NO3 reached the upper end of the scales within 1 - 2 minutes. All the surrounding fields are arable with barley or bearded wheat being grown. The sampling site is around 150 yards downstream of Alveston sewage works (currently being upgraded). If this discharges into the stream it is probably contributing almost all of the flow given recent weather. This stream flows into Tockington Mill Rhine. Water looked clear, some weed growth on bottom, but no obvious symptoms of the high nutrient levels.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: Water level dropping after two days of spate, but still higher than average. Spate peaked in excess of 60cm on Damery gauge but was at 44cm when sample was taken. Water looked brownish due to suspended solids and was the most turbid I have sampled on the Little Avon, but even with the Secchi tube completely full the disc was still just about discernible. There were clots of dirty foam gathered up above snags - see attached photo - though not at my sampling point. I saw a dipper about 100m above the sampling point.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: This small stream runs along the bottom of a shallow valley. Fields on the near (right hand) bank are ripening rape. Fields on far (left hand) bank are sweetcorn. Both phosphate and nitrate tests developed very rapidly (had reached maximum on the chart in around half the allowed time - as happened last year) The sampling point is around 200 m downstream of sewage works. Unaware if it discharges into stream or not.

notes: This is a very small stream (30-50cm across) running through a shallow bowl of fields  given over to ripening dwarf wheat/barley. There is a sewage works located a few hundred yards upstream of the sampling point but I don't know if it discharges into the

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: This is a very small stream (30-50cm across) running through a shallow bowl of fields  given over to ripening dwarf wheat/barley. There is a sewage works located a few hundred yards upstream of the sampling point but I don't know if it discharges into the stream. I did a turbidity test which indicated < 12 ntu. The water looked 'crystal clear' in the stream but in the Secchi tube looked yellow/brown. The stream becomes Tockington Mill Rhine a mile or so downstream of the sampling point. The colour change in the testing was very rapid and exceeded 10ppm for NO3 within 1min 40s and 1ppm for PO4 within 3 minutes, suggesting that the true values were well above the upper threshold of the tests.

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: A stream runs through Druid Woods & the gardens, goes underground  & joins the River Trym not far from the Trym/River Avon junction. The flow depends on rainfall although there is always some flow even in summer. On sampling day the flow was substantial, quite a lot of green algae is present & a lot of insect activity taking place, mossies, dragon flies, lace wings, bees & wasps. Sorry dropped my phone so no photo today.

notes: Sample just upstream from wooden bridge. Stream banks are verdant, but bed is uniformly brown with little signs of plant life or fish.  There are eels in the stream and lots of damsel flies.  The brown coating of the stones on the bed is believed to be ca

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: Sample just upstream from wooden bridge. Stream banks are verdant, but bed is uniformly brown with little signs of plant life or fish.  There are eels in the stream and lots of damsel flies.  The brown coating of the stones on the bed is believed to be caused by fucoxanthin diatoms attached to surfaces. We had a warm dry spring, followed by rapid water flow for a few weeks as the lake upstream was drained for summer dredging.  The flow is now very low.

notes: This is a very small stream which runs into/becomes Tockington Mill Rhine. Sampling point 250m below sewage works. Stream runs along the bottom of a bowl shaped valley between Rudgeway and Old Down. This year the surrounding fields are growing field beans

mncat_name: Avon Bristol and Somerset North Streams
notes: This is a very small stream which runs into/becomes Tockington Mill Rhine. Sampling point 250m below sewage works. Stream runs along the bottom of a bowl shaped valley between Rudgeway and Old Down. This year the surrounding fields are growing field beans. Sampling point by footpath bridge over stream very overgrown this year with nettles, hogweed and greater willow herb. The water looks crystal clear but as in the previous two years, the chemical tests reach the top of the scale in around half of the prescribed reaction time.
